title: Analyses of the Value of the Children as Soldiers split
display_title: Analyses of the Value of the Children as Soldiers split
wikitext:
[]: Analyses of the Value of the Children as Soldiers [split]             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Apr 10, 2014 4:04 pm Split from "Masculine Protest" -NemZ While Rei might be a soldier, albeit fairly robotic, Asuka is more a warrior; though her one command role showed that she was not all individual prowess over teamwork. A few misplaced fragments: SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Apr 10, 2014 4:23 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: *snip*

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Apr 10, 2014 4:36 pm Kendrix wrote: Rei is NOT indifferent towards Shinji .

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Apr 10, 2014 4:45 pm Bagheera wrote: FTFY. The fact she gives a shit about one guy

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Apr 10, 2014 4:51 pm Kendrix wrote: Bull. Touji and Asuka herself (ep 22) are not "one guy". That's nearly all the people Rei ever came into regular contact with.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Thu Apr 10, 2014 4:53 pm Kendrix wrote: Rei's probably the one person who shows the MOST concern about her comrades (including Touji and Asuka),

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Apr 10, 2014 5:07 pm That's not a misrepresentation, it's the sad truth. Even if YOU would find it "rational" to treat Rei like Asuka treated her, Asuka was simply not acting out of rationality. She didn't file a formal protest with Misato or talk about unit cohesion, or call her out and calmly tell her what her problem with her was. She physically launched herself into someone's face and kept snarking everytime they spoke, and tried to get Shinji to gossip with her about her while she was in the room. Name one thing that could make you reasonably expect she's the type who'll just leave you to die... just from episodes 9 to 11. And not studying them with an analytic gaze either, living through them like any other day. Don't assume your perceptionis the only possible one. It's quite possible for someone to go, "Well, that's a weirdo." and not pay her any more mind... which was what actually happened in early ep 9. War wasn't formally declared untill after the synchronization training incident. Sure, she's obedient to her boss when we first see her. In a job where she does close combat. After said boss raised her . (or did the closet thing anyone did at all) Ritsuko and Miato are presented as hypercompetent and Asuka as uber confident when we first see them. We then get a more differentiated picture over time. But whenever a situation where another person's in danger actually arose, we saw what we did see, and it wasn't mechanical indifference. She even cared to advise someone who had treated her with nothing but contempt when they were having trouble, and got slapped and called names for her trouble. but never seem to recall that Rei hesitated for exactly the same reason when she saw the entry plug (which is why she was defeated, of course).

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Apr 10, 2014 5:18 pm Kendrix wrote: Even if YOU would find it "rational" to treat Rei like Asuka treated her, Asuka was simply not acting out of rationality.

[]:             Quote Post by Rei IV » Thu Apr 10, 2014 5:47 pm I say, Bags does make a legitimate point. Rei's a solider to THE CORE and it's really understandable to see why folks do find her ridiculous loyalty/obedience questionable (and disturbing). I mean, really, we're talking about some one who you may have to have SOME inmate connection with in order for her to care otherwise, you can get maimed/killed in the battlefield and she wouldn't even blink. How is that NOT bothersome? Honestly Kendrix, you're a bit too defensive. It's a completely legitimate a point. It's also true that outside Shinji, she doesn't care much for anything outside Gendo's priorities.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Apr 10, 2014 9:00 pm Can't agree with you there, Rei IV. As pwhodges already pointed out (and then was summarily ignored), Rei shows clear concern for the lives of others: She was directly ordered to "stop the target", but hesitated because she didn't want to kill Touji. That's pretty much exactly the opposite of Bagheera's claim about what she would do. Rei's not the type to leave her comrades behind whatsoever; in the scenario Bagheera described, I find it more likely that Rei would save him even if she'd been ordered otherwise.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Thu Apr 10, 2014 10:49 pm Okay, done for the day, so let's see what's up.... All right, I agree with pwhodges, Ed Monk, and Kendrix that Rei showed concern for her fellow pilots (unit first), which is a standing order in any military unit, believe it or not (usually on the principle of leave no one behind, dead or alive). And, no, it's highly doubtful that (back to a military situation, so let's assume that, since that's how I started this catfight...sorry) she would follow illegal orders, because they are illegal. The nuance I was talking about was the layers of orders that a Soldier explicitly and implicitly follows. If he is given an order by his superior, he has to follow it...assuming it's square with the Laws of War, the Rules of Engagement, and the UCMJ (in no particular order). Soldiers memorize their operational parameters so that if they receive a questionable order, that order has become invalidated. Rei, being who she is, would not follow orders that disobeyed the higher parameter. And among those orders are protecting and safeguarding the safety of your fellow Soldiers by all means necessary. I can tell you that I have worked with guys like Rei before. There aren't many of them, and yeah, they are intimidating (and...frankly quite scary). They are also among the best Soldiers I have worked with, and every single guy who knew them would rather work with someone who was distant, cold, unsocial, but knew their job and did it well (which meant everyone AROUND them succeeded by proximity...in a combat situation, that's increased survival), then someone who was a Spotlight Ranger (or a Blue Falcon...that's a fun little acronym). You will find that the most successful Soldiers tend to be the ones who wax more Rei than Asuka. Asuka is a warrior...but she's not a soldier. And there is a difference, because a warrior is a mentality, but a soldier is a profession. Let me share an anecdote about what a 'charge first' mentality is like. I was in a training exercise where I was part of a squad, and we were assaulting a point. We were in a line on a very exposed bern, trying to pin down an OPFOR that had us under fire. We found it, and as me and the rest of the line (in communication) began to focus our fire, some impatient Leroy Jenkins up and ran charging at the point, firing his weapon. He got the OPFOR...by running across three friendly lines of fire, including my own, as I was taking aim on the target and getting ready to squeeze the trigger. If that had been live combat (or, dear God, a live-fire exercise), we would have all committed fratricide because one guy was too gung ho and not patient enough to communicate to us that he was going to go bolting into a shared killzone. A warrior mentality without a professional focus is hazardous. Too everyone around that 'warrior.' I have to reiterate, Asuka is my favorite character, and I sympathize with her far more than the other characters: but as she is when she starts the series, and as she develops? No, I would never trust my life to someone like her. Especially someone who aggressively goes after their coworkers like that. Hell, at the least that's a hostile work environment. If you go after your coworkers because they feel off? It doesn't matter how justified you are, you have done a good job of putting yourself in the wrong. Now, as for Rei: part of her character development was the gradual revelation that she was not emotionless, or uncaring. We saw that she did care about people, as best as she could. And lest we assume that she only cared about Shinji, who was it that saved Asuka from Arael? Rei did. .....this might be turning into a new thread....

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Apr 10, 2014 11:16 pm Bagheera wrote: Her entire conversation with Asuka in the elevator scene lays it all on the table, really.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Thu Apr 10, 2014 11:29 pm Thank you kindly, Kendrix (fantastic follow-up essay, I have to say). Though I wasn't referring specifically to the Spear. While it was the Spear that killed Arael, Rei had that moment of shock when she realized Asuka was being attacked, and she counterattacked first with the positron rifle. It was only after she this that she was ordered to retrieve the Spear. I seem to remember her demeanor changing when that order was given (going from, "Holy crap, Asuka's being eaten" to emotionless "Wonder Girl" when she went for the Spear...but I could be wrong). Also, I've enjoyed your rebuttals, Bagheera. I've noticed the tone of the conversation getting a bit tense for everyone, so I want to reiterate that I'm having a blast discussing this (and having the holes in my arguments pointed out!)

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Apr 10, 2014 11:47 pm Rewatched it. I don't think Rei got a reaction shot (or many face shots at all, mostly zerogoki glittering in the epic music - and a creepy shot like Lillith is actually staring at her before she pulls out the lance), but she grinds her teeth when firing the positron rifle after Arael/ this does have a greater probability of being not just concentration but tenseness about poor Asuka having her brain fried. She's also notably looking in her direction when she was her outburst in the classroom after Touji got selected. Speaking Ep 14 also has a prime example of Asuka knowing she's going after someone who can't defend themselves that much, because of that. "Why don't that idiot just relax!" "He's probably not capable of it..." "That's why he's an idiot!" I'd also point out that while I wanted to make the point that Asuka was simply ugly to her teammates at times, that doesn't mean that... ...the course of this conversation has led to me pointing out a lot of bad stuff about her, but I'd really wish she could relax and allow herself to be that cool, spontaneous/practical girl she is with Hikari and some of the scenes before things got too charged with Shinji. Much like the other characters, you can't take at face value hat she thinks she is, she's not that crying kid, not fully, she'd just a teenaged girl and if she could relax know that this is totally ok, she'd probably ralize that she's got some things in common with her fellow pilots and be a family with them and Misato, and possibl Kaji if he's still alive in the scenario in question. I know she doesn't like, have a motivation to deliberately do evil, she sees herself as faced with a threat of eradication and uneccesaryness/self-preservation, in the state of a beast. As I said before, she's basically this naive girl in an unfair world, pretending to be something else. It's pretty damn unfair, as it is for all of the pilots, really. Ep 26points out a lot of similarities in what fundamentally drives them. It needn't be that... antagonistic, when you talk about the three of them.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Apr 11, 2014 2:46 am Monk Ed wrote: Can't agree with you there, Rei IV. As pwhodges already pointed out (and then was summarily ignored), Rei shows clear concern for the lives of others: She was directly ordered to "stop the target", but hesitated because she didn't want to kill Touji. That's pretty much exactly the opposite of Bagheera's claim about what she would do. Rei's not the type to leave her comrades behind whatsoever; in the scenario Bagheera described, I find it more likely that Rei would save him even if she'd been ordered otherwise.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Apr 11, 2014 3:40 am Bagheera wrote: I wouldn't want to work with her as an LEO, let alone in a combat capacity. And I have personal experience telling me how much other LEOs freak out when they hear someone might be suicidal

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Fri Apr 11, 2014 4:17 am Further, she is not suicidal. Perhaps nihilistic, but not suicidal: her self-destructive actions make sense in light of her circumstances (like a soldier throwing himself on a grenade). As for the law enforcement comparison, that is not really comparable to the situation in the show (a total war of attrition). That requires a completely different mindset for success than law enforcement. A good LEO is essentially a counselor and peace officer (trying to talk through things). Those skills have a place in a combat organization, but not at the Pilots' level.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Apr 11, 2014 4:46 am NemZ wrote: Then again, other people wouldn't know all these peculiarities so reacting to such statements negatively does make sense. It just isn't actually warranted in this case.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Fri Apr 11, 2014 7:55 pm I don't deny that Rei would be terrible as an LEO. I said that. But I wasn't saying she was a good cop: I was saying she was a good soldier. There is a wide gulf of difference in what goes into making a good cop and a good soldier. We have cops in my unit: they talk about the differences. While both professions are certainly dangerous and require very specific mentalities to approach, warfare is not law enforcement. And a Rei is the kind of person best suited to warfare. You are correct in saying she would be bad backup as a cop. She is perfect as a soldier, though. As for Rei's 'leisurely' movement with the Spear, that can be interpreted many ways: such as 'Well, I've removed the lock keeping this thing chained down, and it's about to disappear forever.' There is so much more behind the removal of the Spear than just saving Asuka, unfortunately, and Rei would know what many of those implications would be (and how they would affect her). ...I apologize, but could we split this thread? I feel like it's going to be difficult to get back on tangent to the topic of 'masculine protest.' I want to talk about that, but I'm enjoying this topic as well. Monk was going to do it but I beat him to it. -NemZ

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Apr 11, 2014 7:57 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: While both professions are certainly dangerous and require very specific mentalities to approach, warfare is not law enforcement. And a Rei is the kind of person best suited to warfare. You are correct in saying she would be bad backup as a cop. She is perfect as a soldier, though.
